mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the Philippines on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the south-east. The Philippines is a Pacific archipelago between Taiwan, Indonesia and Vietnam 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the Philippines?
mapQuestions.answer: Manila, on the island of Luzon. The Manila metro area has about 25 million people, one of the densest big cities on Earth.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does the Philippines speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Filipino (based on Tagalog) and English are both official. Most Filipinos also speak one of 180+ local languages (Cebuano, Ilocano, Hiligaynon and others). Most Filipinos are happy chatting in English with visitors.

mapQuestions.question: How many islands does the Philippines have?
mapQuestions.answer: OVER 7,000! 7,641 to be exact (counted at low tide!). About 2,000 are inhabited. The biggest are Luzon (where Manila is) and Mindanao.

mapQuestions.question: Why is the Philippines so Catholic?
mapQuestions.answer: The Philippines was a Spanish colony for over 300 years (1565 to 1898), and Spain spread Catholicism so completely that the Philippines today is around 80% Roman Catholic, the only majority-Christian country in Asia along with East Timor.

foods.name: Adobo
foods.description: Chicken or pork simmered in soy sauce, vinegar, garlic, peppercorns and bay leaves. The unofficial national dish, every Filipino family has its own recipe.

foods.name: Lechon
foods.description: A whole pig spit-roasted slowly over coals until the skin is impossibly crispy. The Filipino party centrepiece. Cebu is famous for the best lechon.

foods.name: Pancit
foods.description: Stir-fried noodles with meat, prawns, vegetables and a squeeze of calamansi (a small green Filipino citrus). Eaten on birthdays for long life.

foods.name: Sinigang
foods.description: A sour tamarind-based soup with pork, prawns or fish and lots of vegetables. The classic Filipino comfort food.

foods.name: Mangoes
foods.description: Filipino mangoes are widely agreed to be the sweetest in the world. Eaten fresh, dried, or as the topping of an ice-cream "halo-halo" dessert.

foods.name: Halo-halo
foods.description: A wild Filipino dessert: shaved ice piled with sweet beans, jellies, fruits, leche flan, ube purple-yam ice-cream, and condensed milk. "Halo-halo" means "mix-mix".

scavengerItems.label: A jeepney (a colourful, hand-painted Philippine minibus made from old US army Jeeps)
A "tricycle" (motorbike with a sidecar) carrying way too many people
A white-sand beach with limestone karst cliffs (Palawan, El Nido, Coron)
Karaoke being sung enthusiastically (Filipinos LOVE karaoke)
A Spanish colonial Catholic church in a town square
A volcano (the Philippines has 23 active ones, including the perfect cone of Mt Mayon)
Coral reefs and clownfish around a Coron or Anilao dive site
A coconut tree growing right out of a beach
The Banaue Rice Terraces (the "eighth wonder of the world", 2,000 years old)
A tarsier in Bohol (huge eyes, tiny body, looks like a Gremlin)
A whale shark (the gentlest giant on Earth) at Donsol or Oslob
A "carabao" (water buffalo) in a rice paddy

geoMatches.description: The capital. Loud, vast, traffic-choked. The Intramuros walled colonial old town is the gentle heart. Most travellers move on quickly.
The most beautiful Philippine island. El Nido and Coron have crystal lagoons, limestone karsts and white sand. World's best beach lists usually feature Palawan.
A small central island famous for the "Chocolate Hills" (over 1,000 perfectly conical hills that turn brown in dry season) and the tiny tarsier primates.
A big central island and major Philippine city. Famous for its crispy lechon roast pork, dive sites, and as one of the first places Magellan reached in 1521.
A mountainous region in northern Luzon with 2,000-year-old rice terraces carved into the mountainsides by the Ifugao people. UNESCO World Heritage.
A small island in the south-east, the surfing capital of the Philippines. Famous for "Cloud 9", one of the best waves in Asia.

senses.smell: Garlic frying in oil for adobo, sweet ripe mango, charcoal smoke from a lechon roast, salt and seaweed at every beach, the petrol-smoke of a jeepney…
senses.hear: Karaoke from every block, "Kumusta!" "Salamat!", the rumble of jeepneys, gentle Filipino English mixed with Tagalog, tropical birds at dawn…
senses.taste: Tangy sour adobo, crispy lechon skin, sour sinigang soup, the world's sweetest mango, the impossible mix of halo-halo…
senses.feel: Soupy tropical humidity, the cool of a karst cliff in shade, soft Palawan sand, the warm Pacific water, the sticky-sweet of a halo-halo spoon…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How many languages are spoken here?
stories.body: The Philippines has more than 180 living languages, spread across 7,000+ islands. Filipino (based on Tagalog) is the national language; English is the second official language and widely used in schools, business and TV. Cebuano (spoken in the central and southern islands) is actually spoken by more people than Tagalog. Other big ones include Ilocano, Hiligaynon, Waray, Bicolano. Many Filipinos speak THREE languages fluently: their regional language, Tagalog/Filipino, and English. Code-switching between English and Tagalog mid-sentence (called "Taglish") is completely normal in everyday conversation in Manila.

stories.question: How were the rice terraces built?
stories.body: About 2,000 years ago, the Ifugao people of northern Luzon began carving rice terraces into the steep mountain walls of the Cordillera. They had no metal tools, just stone and wood. Over generations, they shaped entire mountainsides into staircase-style rice paddies, each one walled in with carefully-fitted stones to hold the water. If you stretched out all the terraces end to end, they would supposedly wrap halfway around the world. The terraces are still farmed today by the Ifugao, although fewer young people want the work. UNESCO put the terraces on the World Heritage list in 1995. Some Filipinos call them the "Eighth Wonder of the World".
stories.thinkingQuestion: These terraces have been worked by the same families for 2,000 years. Are there family responsibilities in your life that go back generations?

stories.question: Was the first round-the-world trip Philippine history?
stories.body: In 1521 the Portuguese explorer Ferdinand Magellan, sailing for Spain, became the first European to reach the Philippine islands. Magellan claimed the islands for Spain. A few weeks later, on the small island of Mactan near Cebu, the local chief Lapu-Lapu and his warriors defeated Magellan in battle. Magellan died in the fight. The rest of his fleet sailed on, with a Basque navigator called Juan Sebastián Elcano, and eventually completed the FIRST round-the-world voyage in history, returning to Spain in 1522. Lapu-Lapu is now a Filipino national hero, with statues across the country. The town on Mactan is named after him.

stories.question: What is a jeepney?
stories.body: A "jeepney" is the iconic Philippine minibus, started after World War II when leftover American Jeeps were modified by Filipino mechanics to carry more passengers. The body was stretched, two long benches were added inside, and the outside was hand-painted in vivid colours, with chrome decorations, prayers and slogans plastered everywhere. For 70 years, jeepneys were the backbone of Philippine public transport. They are now slowly being phased out for cleaner modern buses (a sad change for many Filipinos, because the old jeepneys are part of national identity). If you ride one, you pass your fare hand-to-hand up to the driver via the other passengers, no electronic ticketing needed.

stories.question: Can you swim with whale sharks?
stories.body: Whale sharks ("butanding" in Filipino) are the largest fish on Earth, up to 12 metres long. They are gentle plankton-eaters, completely harmless. Two places in the Philippines (Donsol in Sorsogon, and Oslob in Cebu) allow tourists to swim with them. Donsol is the more responsible operation (the whale sharks come in for the season naturally); Oslob is more controversial because the sharks are attracted with food (which scientists say can affect their normal behaviour). Floating in clear water beside an animal the size of a school bus, watching it glide past with that huge mouth half-open, is one of the most extraordinary wildlife experiences anywhere on Earth.

convoQuestions.question: Beach, island, mountain, city, which has felt the most "Philippines"?
Did you see a jeepney in action?
How would you feel about living on an island country with 7,000 islands?
The Banaue rice terraces have been farmed for 2,000 years. What does that kind of continuity feel like to you?

wordSearch.hint: Filipino foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Carabao (water buffalo)
animals.description: The national animal. Big, slow, and used for ploughing rice paddies across the country.

animals.name: Philippine tarsier
animals.description: Tiny nocturnal primate with HUGE eyes. Lives in Bohol's rainforest. Only as big as your fist.

animals.name: Philippine eagle
animals.description: The national bird, one of the largest and rarest eagles on Earth. Lives only on Mindanao. Critically endangered.

animals.name: Clownfish
animals.description: In coral reefs in Anilao, Apo Island, Bohol. Made famous by Finding Nemo, still everywhere on Philippine reefs.

animals.name: Whale shark (butanding)
animals.description: The biggest fish on Earth, gentle plankton-eater. Swimming with them at Donsol or Oslob is one of Asia's great wildlife encounters.

animals.name: Saltwater crocodile
animals.description: In the Agusan Marsh, Mindanao. Rare in the Philippines but the world's largest reptile species.

animals.name: Mindanao bleeding-heart pigeon
animals.description: A ground-dwelling pigeon with a red splash of feathers on its chest that looks like a bleeding heart. Endangered, found only in the Philippines.
